title: Forest Plot Data
tagline: Extract per-study effect sizes + variances from a meta-analysis paper
description: Pulls a meta-analysis's per-study table into a CSV-friendly format: one row per included study with id, yi (effect size), and vi (variance), plus optional context columns (year, sample size, subgroup). The output drops directly into Metalens for interactive forest-plot exploration.
task_description: Extract per-study effect sizes (yi) and variances (vi) from a meta-analysis paper, one row per included study, ready for forest-plot tools.
context: Meta-analysis papers ‚Äî looking for the included-studies table or forest plot. Each row needs an id (typically author + year), yi (effect size on the metric the meta-analysis pools), and vi (variance, computed from SE or CI when not reported directly).
prompt:
You are extracting study-level effect-size data from a meta-analysis paper. The output will be used to build an interactive forest plot, so it MUST contain one row per primary study that the meta-analysis included.

# Goal

Return exactly ONE JSON object containing:
- ``paper_metadata`` ‚Äî the meta-analysis paper's own identifying metadata: ``{"title": ..., "doi": ..., "year": ..., "authors": [...]}``. Extract from the title page / front matter / header / footer. ``title`` is required (never empty); ``doi``, ``year``, ``authors`` use null if not determinable.
- ``metric`` ‚Äî a short string describing the effect-size metric the meta-analysis pools (e.g. ``"log_OR"``, ``"log_RR"``, ``"log_HR"``, ``"SMD"``, ``"raw_MD"``, ``"r"``, ``"Hedges_g"``)
- ``studies`` ‚Äî a ``_table``-wrapped array, one row per included primary study
- ``evidence`` ‚Äî verbatim snippet support for each row (see Evidence section below)

Ignore meta-regressions, sensitivity analyses, subgroup pooled estimates, and any "overall" / "summary" rows that aren't a primary study.

# Per-study fields

**Required (every row):**
- ``id``           ‚Äî a unique study identifier. Prefer ``"Author Year"`` (e.g. ``"Smith 2018"``); if multiple studies share author+year, suffix with a letter (``"Smith 2018a"``, ``"Smith 2018b"``). Fall back to ``"study1"``, ``"study2"``, ‚Ä¶ only if no usable label is printed.
- ``yi``           ‚Äî the effect size estimate as reported by the meta-analysis, on the metric listed in ``metric``. Numeric.
- ``vi``           ‚Äî the variance of ``yi``. Numeric. If only SE is reported: ``vi = SE^2``. If only a 95 % CI is reported on the same metric: ``SE = (upper - lower) / (2 * 1.96)``, then ``vi = SE^2``. For ratio-scale effects (OR / RR / HR) the meta-analysis usually pools the log of the ratio ‚Äî convert by ``yi = log(ratio)`` and use the CI on the log scale (``log(upper)`` / ``log(lower)``) when computing SE.

**Optional (include when reported):**
- ``year``         ‚Äî publication year of the primary study (integer)
- ``n``            ‚Äî total sample size of the primary study
- ``n_treatment``  ‚Äî treatment / intervention group size (when applicable)
- ``n_control``    ‚Äî control / comparison group size (when applicable)
- ``country``      ‚Äî country / region of the primary study
- ``design``       ‚Äî study design (RCT, observational, cross-sectional, cohort, etc.)
- ``subgroup``     ‚Äî any moderator label the meta-analysis groups studies by (e.g. age range, intervention type)
- ``weight``       ‚Äî the meta-analysis's reported weight for this study, as a percentage
- ``notes``        ‚Äî anything else worth flagging for the analyst (e.g. "variance imputed from CI")

# Where to look (in order of reliability)

1. The **forest plot** itself ‚Äî read the per-row labels and numeric estimates / CIs directly off the figure.
2. An **included-studies table** with one row per study (often Table 1 or 2; sometimes in supplementary materials).
3. The **appendix** or supplementary materials ‚Äî many meta-analyses publish the full per-study extraction there.
4. The **methods section** ‚Äî useful for understanding which metric is pooled and how variances were computed.

If the paper is itself a primary study (not a meta-analysis), return ``"studies": {"_table": []}`` and add a single note explaining the document type.

# Output format (strict)

```
{
  "paper_metadata": {"title": "A meta-analysis of ‚Ä¶", "doi": "10.1037/abc.0000123", "year": 2022, "authors": ["Smith J", "Jones K"]},
  "metric": "<metric-name>",
  "studies": {
    "_table": [
      {"id": "Smith 2018", "yi": -0.42, "vi": 0.052, "year": 2018, "n": 312, "design": "RCT", "notes": ""},
      {"id": "Jones 2020", "yi":  0.13, "vi": 0.021, "year": 2020, "n": 488, "design": "cohort", "notes": "vi computed from 95 % CI"}
    ]
  },
  "evidence": [
    {"snippet": "Smith et al. 2018 ... RR 0.66 (95 % CI 0.42-1.04)", "page": 4, "source": "Figure 2", "field": "studies._table[0]"},
    {"snippet": "Jones (2020): adjusted HR 1.14, 95 % CI 1.00-1.30",     "page": 5, "source": "Table 1", "field": "studies._table[1]"}
  ]
}
```

Return ONLY this JSON object ‚Äî no markdown fences, no prose before or after, no comments. Use ``null`` for any optional field you can't determine.

# Evidence

For every study row, include at least ONE entry in ``evidence`` whose ``snippet`` is the verbatim text (forest-plot row label, table cell, or appendix line) that supports the row's ``yi`` / ``vi``. The ``field`` value should be the JSON-path of the row it supports (e.g. ``"studies._table[3]"``).

Also include one ``evidence`` entry whose ``snippet`` is the verbatim caption of the forest plot or included-studies table you read from ‚Äî the viewer uses this caption to find and highlight the figure/table region. The caption entry can use ``"field": "studies"``.

Snippet rules:
- Quote character-for-character; do NOT paraphrase.
- Pages are 1-indexed PDF pages, not journal page numbers.
- If you cannot recover a verbatim snippet for a row (e.g. the value is read off a forest-plot bar with no numeric label), omit the evidence for that row rather than fabricating a quote.
